title: What Is Exchange-Traded Fund? Definition, Formula & Examples
author: Sigma Capital
metaDescription: What is Exchange-Traded Fund? Learn the definition, formula, and real-world examples of ETF. Our comprehensive guide explains how ETF works and why it matters for investors.
excerpt: An ETF is a pooled investment vehicle that holds a basket of securities — such as stocks, bonds, or commodities — and trades on a stock exchange like a single stock. ETFs offer instant diversification...

content.definition: An ETF is a pooled investment vehicle that holds a basket of securities — such as stocks, bonds, or commodities — and trades on a stock exchange like a single stock. ETFs offer instant diversification, low expense ratios (often 0.03-0.20%), tax efficiency, and intraday liquidity. They have revolutionized investing by making professional-grade diversification accessible to everyone with as little as $1 through fractional shares.
content.formula: ETF Price = Net Asset Value (NAV) of Underlying Holdings / Shares Outstanding
content.example: SPY (SPDR S&P 500 ETF) holds all 500 stocks in the S&P 500 index. Buying one share gives you proportional exposure to all 500 companies. If the S&P 500 rises 10%, SPY rises approximately 10% minus its tiny 0.09% expense ratio.
content.types: Broad-market ETFs (VTI, SPY) cover entire markets. Sector ETFs (XLK, XLF) target specific industries. Bond ETFs (BND, AGG) provide fixed-income exposure. Commodity ETFs (GLD, SLV) track physical commodities. International ETFs (VXUS, EFA) offer global diversification.
content.whyItMatters: Understanding ETF is essential for making informed investment decisions. Whether you are evaluating individual stocks, planning your portfolio allocation, or comparing investment options, ETF provides a quantitative framework that removes guesswork and helps you assess value objectively. Professional investors, analysts, and financial advisors all rely on ETF as a core metric in their decision-making process.

image.alt: Wooden tiles spelling ETF growth on a wooden surface, symbolizing investment strategy.
